We also observed many Xylocopa individuals foraging on the same plants (at least two species). On 7/4/2024, there were also B. crotchii at this location, observed by USFS, Drew Farr.

weatherNotes: It felt warm in the sun, humidity was high, from 2-3:30pm the cloud layer increased dramatically until the sky was overcast, but still bright.
managementNotes: We used a private road and access trails to walk a slope and hillside that are ~200m from a barn, stables, and areas actively grazed by cattle. There are honey bee keepers in the suburban community below the survey point, and honey bees are brought in frequently for work in nearby orchards, the closest orchards are some 500-700m away. I know the orchards use herbicides and insecticides, but those are not used on the property where the survey was undertaken.

floralHostNotes: The specific plants it was foraging on were in early bloom and were producing copious nectar, because of the duration of foraging on each flower.  Other nearly plants are just forming floral whorls, while others have not differentiated flowers on growing tips yet.  It will be a long bloom period for the black sage this year, which  means extended food availability for foragers, provided high temperatures do not cut it short.
occurrenceRemarks: I was slow-mo filming honeybee field bees foraging on black sage flowers, when the unmistakable sound of a bumble bee diverted my attention.  I was delightfully surprised to see a species of bumble bee I had not seen before and enjoyed identifying it with my wife back at the house.  We were surprised by the color variation in this species and pleased to see the distinctive reddish tergal segments 4-5 of this individual.

managementNotes: Verbena Fields is a 17-acre former gravel mine on the Lindo Channel flood plain, "lightly" managed as a native habitat open space with creekbed, meadow, oak woods, and mowed and gravel paths. Native American ritual burning is practiced. I focused on riparian and creek-adjacent area which is unmowed and has a nice variety of native and non-native plants. Surrounding cultivated gardens are also resources for bees. Saw many species of bees native and non-. Counted 14 species in bloom and several more not flowered yet. Also oak, cottonwoods, sycamores, ash, walnut.
ecoregion: Central California Valley

floralHostNotes: Quite a decent patch of this growing along the north shore of Crystal Lake. Along with some blooming Acmispon and Asclepias fascicularis, and perhaps a bit of non-blooming Artemisia dracunculus.
occurrenceRemarks: Wasn't expecting to find so many bees here. Was about to get our nets and do impromptu point survey, but spotted the beginnings of the Fork fire, and not knowing how far away it was, decided we'd better just leave, but here is what I photoed in just 5 minutes. Estimate a few dozen Bombus here. Did not notice any Apis mellifera.
ecoregion: Southern California Mountains
